topic: Areas of Inquiry: Music for Listening and Performance
subject: music

items.q: What is characteristic of Baroque music?
items.correct: Continuous bass line, ornamentation, terraced dynamics, and counterpoint.
items.why: Students project later styles backwards. Baroque (1600-1750) used basso continuo, harpsichord/organ, and terraced (sudden) dynamics. Piano hadn't yet replaced harpsichord.

items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.q: Whose 1953 Concentus Musicus Wien revived historically informed Baroque playing?
items.why: Correct answer: Nikolaus Harnoncourt. “John Eliot Gardiner” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Christopher Small. “Susan McClary” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Glenn Gould abandon live performance?
items.why: Exact answer: 1964 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
